posts.name: Eric Dinnocenzo
posts.cooked:
I purchased from a wine retailer a case of 2011 La Spinona Barbaresco Bricco Faset - and most of the bottles have an oxidized smell and taste. They are not really drinkable.

Of course, the wine could have been stored improperly.

But … does this surprise the Barolo/Barbaresco lovers here?

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
ericdinn:

La Spinona Barbaresco

Yes, that’s surprising. While 2011 was a very difficult year in Barolo (rain, hail), it was much better in Barbaresco. La Spinona is traditional in some ways (e.g., large oak cask aging) but the Faset only stays on the skins 12-15 days, which is much shorter than the traditional 21+ days, so maybe it lacked some structure that would have preserved it.

But if you bought the case recently, storage would be the most likely suspect. That was probably released 10 years ago.

link_counts.title: Azienda Agricola La Spinona Barbaresco - Barbaresco Bricco Faset DOCG

posts.name: Benjamin Hand
posts.cooked:
I’ve had several problems in recent years buying Barolo and Barbaresco on the secondary market and discovering that the bottles seem prematurely oxidized (to varying degrees). These weren’t top wines, which might account for lower quality storage, but I’ve also wondered if moderate premature oxidation stands out more with Nebbiolo than with other age-worthy red wines.

Perhaps not, and it was just a run of poor luck on my part, but I’ve had worse luck with Barolo and Bararesco than secondary market Bordeaux, Burgundy, and Rhone wines of the same general price point.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
I’ve encountered a few surprisingly oxidized nebbiolos from producers who relied heavily on barriques: Musso Barbaresco Rio Sordo 1999 (completely shot in 2002; dumped) and 2004 (2 of 4 bottles quite oxidized, one as early as 2011), the 2004 Ghisolfi normale Barolo (a trace of oxidation in two bottles in 2019 and 2024).

A 1996 Aldo Conterno Colonello in 2015 was very oxidized and other people have had similar issues with A. Conterno in that period. (This was discussed in threads a few years ago.) Though they claimed to adhere to traditional methods, some of their wines in the 90s and early 2000s seemed oaky. Another bottle last year was kind of tired – odd for a '96 – but not oxidized.

These were all purchased on release.

posts.name: Eric Dinnocenzo
posts.cooked: I purchased a 1997 Aldo Conterno Colonello at auction and it was oxidized. Like another poster said above, I have bought a number of Barolos in auction that were oxidized and not drinkable. Some that come to mind are a 2010 Marcarini Brunate, 2013 Sottimano Barbaresco Pajore and Fausoni, and a 2013 Maria do a run. I forget the others. In any case, I have co caused that buying Nebbiolo at auction is increasingly risky with the age on the bottle.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Which auctions? I wouldn’t expected oxidized 2010s or 2013s.

posts.name: Tom Gutting
posts.cooked: Yeah, this really surprises me for wines that young. I’d be inclined to think storage conditions … but did they tasted stewed or heat damaged at all?

posts.name: Eric Dinnocenzo
posts.cooked:
It is possible I am misclassifying the wine as oxidized rather than stewed or overheated. Admittedly, I am not an expert at classifying wine defects. I can, however, say the bottles were not corked.

The 2011 Spinona had a bit of a burnt brown color at the edges, an unpleasant and almost sour nose, and the tannins and acidity were diminished. The taste was funky and off. I cannot, at this moment, give a better description of the taste. One often hears the comparison to carmelized apple - maybe. I can say for sure the wine did not lose taste. It had a taste - an unpleasant one. An overcooked taste - maybe.

I related it to oxidation because I have had much older wines that had a bit of the same qualities, but these 2011 and 2013s had it much more severely. It made me think about cork sealing quality and aging variability among wines. Also, now that I think of it, not long ago, I purchased a 6 bottle wood case of Siepi at auction and 1 bottle had these same defects and the others were fine

posts.name: Benjamin Hand
posts.cooked: Yes, I’ve had several bottles of mid-priced Barolo and Barbaresco purchases on the secondary market recently from vintages 2006 - 2014 that weren’t stewed fruit and weren’t obviously corked, but instead seemed oxidized and/or prematurely aged. I assume it must be some combination of cork quality and storage conditions. My question is why my anecdotal experience is that it happens more with these than the Bordeaux and Burgundy that I buy from the same retailer. One answer is just bad luck. Another is that consumer are less likely to store these wines with the same care. Another answer would be that cork quality is worse and/or something about the finished wine makes it more susceptible to these issues if and when there is poor storage or cork issues.

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked:
ericdinn:

bit of a burnt brown color at the edges

Keep in mind that is typical for Nebbiolo, especially when made in large wood vessels.

ericdinn:

almost sour nose

I think this is somewhat common as well, at least in comparison to other grapes that tend to have a fruitier smell.

ericdinn:

tannins and acidity were diminished

Typical to a degree with 2011 Piedmont.

With all that said, I am not saying your wine isn’t flawed. If anything it sounds a little cooked.

posts.name: Rob Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
This isn’t a producer I have experience with, but I think oxidation with Nebbiolo is an interesting topic. Storage and shipping conditions may be the #1 culprit (Italy is certainly not known for good storage of any wines that stay in Italy), and historically that’s what I always assumed when I had an oxidized bottle, but there is some evidence that it may be more than that. There’s a thread on Vinous about how the 2010 Bartolo Mascarello may be prematurely oxidizing. I didn’t buy mine on release so I can’t be assured of storage conditions, but 2 of the 4 I’ve opened so far have been oxidized. Several other posters who bought on release mentioned experiencing an oxidized bottle of that wine.

When I did a 2010 tasting a few weeks ago, I opened 2 bottles of Elvio Cogno Pernice from the same original carton and 1 was oxidized and browned on the edges, 1 was excellent and youthful in color - can’t be explained by storage/shipping.

It’s possible there are some premox-like issues with Nebbiolo, at least for producers that continue to use tiny corks. Maybe those tiny corks worked in the past but are no longer adequate for some reason. There’s much less coverage on Piedmont than Burgundy, so it’s possible this is an issue and there just hasn’t been coverage - hard to say. We need a Piedmont @William_Kelley to investigate…

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked:
I had a couple very tired Bartolo bottles. 08 IIRC. Decided not to buy more after that.

I imported a producer that still used the smaller corks at least through 2013. So far out of roughly a dozen btls between 2012 and 2013 over the last year there have been no issues. Both vintages are about where I would expect them to be.

posts.name: Eric Johnson
posts.cooked: I bought two bottles of 2013 Pio Cesare Barbaresco from total wine a while back. I wasn’t expecting anything great, but they were both horrendous and had massive oxidative notes to them. Contrasting that to a Produttori I’d opened a couple of weeks earlier and it was incredibly disappointing.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Quite simply natural cork not doing its job. DIAM or screwcap please!

John_Morris:

La Spinona is traditional in some ways (e.g., large oak cask aging) but the Faset only stays on the skins 12-15 days, which is much shorter than the traditional 21+ days, so maybe it lacked some structure that would have preserved it.

I’ve always like this producer since the early 1990s as a lower-priced, under-the-radar quality producer. While I understand that you are speculating, I can’t see having a week’s less maceration being a factor in showing poorly after only thirteen years from harvest.

posts.name: Andrew L.
posts.cooked: Not this particular wine, but have been noticing this in our tasting circle. Wines purchased on release, stored meticulously and then absolutely shot at 10-15 years from vintage when opened.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I have mentioned this previously but I don’t think everyone in the chain is handling stuff properly. I have had problems with 2013 and now 2016 Produttori wines that were purchased on release from a very reputable retailer that have been universally shot. Somewhere they got cooked. I suspect that’s what happened to the OP’s 2011s.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
David_Bu3ker:

I have mentioned this previously but I don’t think everyone in the chain is handling stuff properly. I have had problems with 2013 and now 2016 Produttori wines that were purchased on release from a very reputable retailer that have been universally shot. Somewhere they got cooked. I suspect that’s what happened to the OP’s 2011s.

The reason I’m quick to suspect this is that I’ve seen a ton of temperature data and it happens much more often than a lot of people here would think.

posts.name: Andrew L.
posts.cooked: I agree there are likely shipping issues, but what is odd is that these wines often show well on release, which is why they got cellared in the first place. I would think any significant heat damage would be evident early on.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
What we don’t know is whether the OP’s bottles were shot from the get go (bad handling during initial import) or something happened later on.

As for damage in general, a heat event that is detrimental to the aging capacity of a wine does not always show at first.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked: Curiously I had an oxidized 2011 Barbaresco a year ago (as documented here). That bottle was bought from a local shop soon after the new vintage hit the shelves and it had been aged in my cellar since. Unless the producer or the importer kept the wine in too hot temperatures at some point, it didn’t have any time to get heat damaged. Don’t know what happened with the wine - a poor cork, perhaps?

posts.name: Lawrence Leichtman
posts.cooked: All of this sounds like bad corkage to me. Perhaps, these wineries got a supply of bad corks.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked: In our last Nebbiolo tasting, back in October, we had a 2014 Burlotto and 2015 Cappellano that was waaay to advanced for their age. My friend had bought them from some serious wine collectors, so doubt they had been stored badly…

posts.name: Eric Dinnocenzo
posts.cooked:
OP here. Could it be that some of these Nebbiolo wines do not age as long as we might think? I mean, it could be corkage issues, but maybe some Barolos and Barbarescos simply do not age that well.

I opened another 2011 Spinona Barbaresco and it did not have that oxidized or stewed taste as the last bottle - and both were purchased from the same sources. It also did not have the unpleasant nose of the other bottle. It did have diminished tannins and acidity like it was past its prime.

I also opened a 2010 Pelissero Barbaresco Vanotu tonight that was purchased at auction and it similarly tastes a little past its prime. Not objectionable, just that the tannins, acidity and fruit all seem diminished.

In contrast, I recently had a 2009 Scavino Bric del Fiasc and it was so tannic that it was not drinkable. I felt like it would need another 10 years in the cellar…

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Otto_Forsberg:

Curiously I had an oxidized 2011 Barbaresco a year ago (as documented here). That bottle was bought from a local shop soon after the new vintage hit the shelves and it had been aged in my cellar since. Unless the producer or the importer kept the wine in too hot temperatures at some point, it didn’t have any time to get heat damaged. Don’t know what happened with the wine - a poor cork, perhaps?

It can happen quickly. I’ve seen pick ups get delayed with wine sitting in high temperatures for multiple days in a loading area at the winery. Once it gets on a truck, all kinds of crazy things can happen, even over fairly short distances. Add in anything intermodal with rail or ship and there’s a lot of potential. Of course, it’s always possible that it’s just a bad cork, or even that the wine was somehow messed up before it got into bottle.

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked:
DougS:

all kinds of crazy things can happen

Crazy stuff does indeed happen: I once received a notice from Hillebrand saying some containers had blown overboard en route across the Atlantic and that my wine might have been in one of those cans. Fortunately it wasn’t but in this instance I learned this isn’t entirely out of the ordinary for these containers to end up in the ocean.

Rarely have I seen heat damage so long as good shippers are used. I’m talking tens of thousands of cases. I have seen it happen but only to an importer skimping and using thermal blanket instead of refrigerated container.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked: It’s immensely less likely with reefer, but it still happens.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: This doesn’t particularly surprise me. When you take an oxidative grape variety, subject it to what are/were frequently oxidative élevage and bottling practices, and then bottle it under a variable closure, that variation is going to be expressed as heterogenous evolution within a lot, with some examples more evolved than others, potentially dramatically so. The more resistant to oxidation the underlying wine, the less noticeable the variation will be.

posts.name: Eric Dinnocenzo
posts.cooked:
I agree. I know for sure I have had wines made in a more oxidative style.

All I can discern is that some Barolo and Barbaresco age better than others, whether due to the grapes used, winemaking process, and/or how well the cork seals.

Of course, you add in the issue of storage as well.

What I conclude is that purchasing aged Barolo and Barbaresco is very random and hit-or-miss, unless you really know what you are buying.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Practices such as frequency of topping, degree of aeration during racking, dissolved oxygen uptake at bottling—along with circumstantial conditions such as cellar temperature during summer—take longer to play out in tannic red wines than they do in more fragile white wines, but they will always count in the longer term.

posts.name: Steve Nellessen
posts.cooked: '11 produttori rabaja today, very good, no sign of oxy or maltreatment. This after several poor asilies and ovelloes over the last year.
